Lab 1: The Apollo Missions¶

Introduction¶

Imagine you are an engineer working on the very early days of the Apollo program. In order to send people to the moon, you need a basic understanding of the gravitational potential and forces the mission will experience. You also need to start quantifying the performance of the new rocket that will carry the capsule: the Saturn V.

Your team at NASA has been tasked with conducting a series of investigations, which you are guided through below. You will make some plots, and also write some short paragraphs with your scientific conclusions. The NASA Director Gene Kranz will use your notebook to defend NASA funding to congress. So your answers should be short and to the point, quantitative in nature, and contain highly polished plots, with appropriate titles, units, etc.

Other important requirements:

  • Your final notebook---BOTH the .ipynb file AND the html export---should be COMMITTED and PUSHED to your fork of the PHYS265-spring26 GitHub repository. Instructions for doing this are available online.
  • All plotting should be done in object oriented fashion, as discussed in class. Any use of “plt.plot()” will result in zero points being awarded for that question.
  • Before submitting your work, reset JLD and then re-run your final code (Kernel → Restart Kernel and Run All Cells…) Make sure your code executes as intended and without error. The first cell should be "1", and the cells should execute in order.

A table of relevant physical constants is below. Good luck!

Quantity Value
Gravitational Constant ($G$) $6.67 \times 10^{-11}$ m³/kg/s²
Gravitational Acceleration ($g$) 9.81 m/s²
Mass of the Earth ($M_E$) $5.9 \times 10^{24}$ kg
Mass of the Moon ($M_M$) $7.3 \times 10^{22}$ kg
Mass of Apollo Command Module 5500 kg
Radius of the Earth ($R_E$) 6378 km
Radius of the Moon ($R_M$) 1737 km
Distance from Earth to Moon ($d_{EM}$) $3.8 \times 10^{8}$ m
Exhaust Velocity of Saturn V Stage 1 (S-1C) ($v_e$) $2.4 \times 10^{3}$ m/s
Burn Rate of S-1C ($\dot{m}$) $1.3 \times 10^{4}$ kg/s
Wet Mass of S-1C ($m_0$) $2.8 \times 10^{6}$ kg
Dry Mass of S-1C ($m_f$) $7.5 \times 10^5$ kg
In [1]:
#show
# In the code cell beneath this one you can put your imports,
# and define any variables you might need (e.g. the mass of the earth)
In [2]:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import scipy

Part 1: The Gravitational Potential of the Earth¶

The gravitational potential at a distance $r$ from a mass $M$ is given by:

$$\Phi(r) = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

where G is the gravitational constant.

  1. Code up a function that takes as an input the mass of a body $M$ , the cartesian location of the mass $(x_M , y_M)$, and the location of some distant evaluation point $(x, y)$. Have the function return the potential at the evaluation point. You may consider approximating the Earth as a point particle, but in which case, be careful! For in that approximation, the the potential contains a singularity. In our function, use all modern best practices for coding. E.g. type hints, type checks, pre and post conditions, doc strings, etc.
In [3]:
def Grav_Pot_2(M : (float,int),x_m : (float,int),y_m : (float,int),x_eval : (float,int,np.ndarray),y_eval : (float,int,np.ndarray)) -> float:
    """A function which calculates the gravitational potential at a some distant point due to a mass located at some other arbitrary point

    Args:
        M (float or int): the mass
        x_m (float or int): the x-coordinate of the mass
        y_m (float or int): the y-coordinate of the mass
        x_eval (float, int, or np.ndarray): the x-coordinate of the point of interest
        y_eval (float, int, or np.ndarray): the y-coordinate of the point of interest

    Returns:
        float: the gravitational potential due to the mass at the point of interest
    """
    if not isinstance(M, (float, int)):
        raise TypeError(f"Mass input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(x_m, (float, int)):
        raise TypeError(f"Test mass x-coordinate input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(y_m, (float, int)):
        raise TypeError(f"Test mass y-coordinate input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(x_eval, (float,int,np.ndarray)):
        raise TypeError(f"Test x-coordinate input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(y_eval, (float,int,np.ndarray)):
        raise TypeError(f"Test y-coordinate input is of the wrong type!")
    else:
        r = np.hypot(x_eval-x_m,y_eval-y_m)
        Phi = np.where(np.isclose(r,0),np.nan,-scipy.constants.G*M/r) #take Earth to be a point particle
        return Phi
  1. Make a 1D plot of the absolute value of the gravitational potential $|\Phi|$ as a function of distance $x$ from the surface of the Earth, assuming $y = 0$. Plot out to $1.5\times$ the distance from the Earth to the Moon. Because the potential falls quickly with r, make the y-axis logarithmic to visualize it better.
In [4]:
#Let the coordinates of the Earth be 0,0
x_params = np.linspace(0,5.7E8,251)
print(type(x_params))
M_E = 5.9E24
print(type(M_E))
#phi_params = Gravitational_Potential(M_E,x_params,0,0,0)
phi_params = np.abs(Grav_Pot_2(M_E,0,0,x_params,0))

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x_params,phi_params)
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Distance from Earth [m]")
ax.set_ylabel("Gravitational Potential [J/kg]")
ax.set_title("Gravitational Potential due to Earth's Gravity with respect to distance")

plt.tight_layout()

  1. Then, make a 2D color-mesh plot of the potential $\Phi$ with the Earth at the origin. Plot $|\Phi|$ over a large range of $x$ and $y$, for example, from $-1.5d_{EM}$ to $+1.5d_{EM}$ . Force the plot to have a square aspect ratio. Make sure to add a nice colorbar, and make the colorbar logarithmic.
In [5]:
xx = np.linspace(-5.7E8,5.7E8,501)
yy = np.linspace(-5.7E8,5.7E8,501)

x_grid, y_grid = np.meshgrid(xx,yy)
z = np.abs(Grav_Pot_2(M_E,0,0,x_grid,y_grid))

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.set_aspect("equal")
mesh = ax.pcolormesh(x_grid, y_grid, z, cmap='rainbow')
mesh.set_norm('log')
cbar = fig.colorbar(mesh)

ax.set_xlabel("x position [m]")
ax.set_ylabel("y position [m]")
cbar.set_label("Gravitational potential [J/kg]")
ax.set_title("Gravitational Potential of the Earth Alone 2D")

plt.tight_layout()

Part 2: The Gravitational Potential of the Earth-Moon System¶

Now, imagine the Earth is at the origin $(x_{E},y_{E}) = (0,0)$, and the moon is at a location $(x_{M},y_{M}) = \big( \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}} \big)$.

  1. Make an updated 2D color-mesh plot, from $-1.5d_{EM}$ to $+1.5d_{EM}$, of the gravitational potential of the combined Earth-Moon system.
In [6]:
dEM = 3.8E8
MM = 7.3E22
x2 = np.linspace(-1.5*dEM, 1.5*dEM,501)
y2 = np.linspace(-1.5*dEM, 1.5*dEM,501)
x_grid2, y_grid2 = np.meshgrid(x2,y2)

pot = np.abs(Grav_Pot_2(M_E,0,0,x_grid2,y_grid2)+Grav_Pot_2(MM,dEM/np.sqrt(2),dEM/np.sqrt(2),x_grid2,y_grid2))

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.set_aspect("equal")
mesh2 = ax.pcolormesh(x_grid2, y_grid2, pot, cmap='viridis')
mesh2.set_norm('log')
cbar = fig.colorbar(mesh2)

ax.set_xlabel("x position [m]")
ax.set_ylabel("y position [m]")
cbar.set_label("Gravitational potential [J/kg]")
ax.set_title("Gravitational Potential of the Earth and Moon 2D")

plt.tight_layout()

  1. Additionally, make a 2D contour plot over the same $x$ and $y$ range. Do not let all the contours bunch up around the Earth and Moon. You will probably again need to tell matplotlib to use a logarithmic color scale.
In [7]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.set_aspect("equal")
contour = ax.contour(x_grid2,y_grid2,np.log(pot),levels=100)
cbar = fig.colorbar(contour)

ax.set_xlabel("x position [m]")

plt.tight_layout()

Part 3: The Gravitational Force Field of the Earth-Moon System¶

The gravitational force $\vec{F}$ that a mass $M_1$ exerts on a mass $m_2$ is:

$$\vec{F}_{21} = -G \frac{M_1 m_2}{|\vec{r}_{21}|^2} \hat{r}_{21}$$

where $\vec{r}_{21}$ is the displacement vector from $M_1$ to $m_2$.

  1. Code up a function that takes as an input the mass of a body $M_1$, the mass of a secondary body $m_2$, the cartesian location of the first mass $(x_1, y_1)$, and the cartesian location of the second body $(x_2, y_2)$. Have the function return the two gravitational force components, $F_x$ and $F_y$, experienced by the mass $m_2$ from the force exerted by $M_1$. Note that the gravitational force contains a singularity, and so if you made the approximation that the Earth is a finite point, you will need to avoid it. Like for the 1D potential, make sure it follows modern best-coding practices.
In [8]:
G = scipy.constants.G
def Grav_Force(M1 : (float, int),M2 : (float, int),x1 : (float, int),y1 : (float,int),x2 : (float,int,np.ndarray),y2 : (float,int,np.ndarray) ) -> (float, float):
    """A function which calculates the gravitational force on a mass M2 at postition (x2,y2) due a some other mass M1 at position (x1,y1)

    Args:
        M1 (float or int): the mass of the first object
        M2 (float or int): the mass of the second object
        x1 (float or int): the x-coordinate of the first object
        y1 (float or int): the y-coordinate of the first object
        x2 (float, int, or np.ndarray): the x-coordinate of the second object
        y2 (float, int, or np.ndarray): the y-coordinate of the second object

    Returns:
        float: the x-component of the gravitational force between the two objects
        float: the y-component of the gravitational force between the two objects
    """
    
    if not isinstance(M1, (float, int)):
        raise TypeError(f"First mass input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(M2, (float, int)):
        raise TypeError(f"Second mass input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(x1, (float, int)):
        raise TypeError(f"First x-coordinate input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(y1, (float, int)):
        raise TypeError(f"First y-coordinate input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(x2, (float,int,np.ndarray)):
        raise TypeError(f"Second x-coordinate input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(y2, (float,int,np.ndarray)):
        raise TypeError(f"Second y-coordinate input is of the wrong type!")
    else:
        r = np.hypot(x2-x1,y2-y1)
        mag_f = G*M1*M2/r**2
        f = np.where(np.isclose(r,0),np.nan,mag_f) #assume point masses
        vec_r = np.array([x2-x1, y2-y1]) #gravitational force is attractive
        vec_r_hat = vec_r/np.linalg.norm(vec_r)
        fx = -f*vec_r_hat[0]
        fy = -f*vec_r_hat[1]
        return fx, fy
  1. Assuming the Earth is still at the origin and the Moon is still at $(x_M, y_M) = \big( \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}} \big)$, make a 2D streamplot of the gravitational force the Earth-Moon system will exert on the Apollo 11 command module. Plot the force over the same x-y range of $-1.5 d_{EM}$ to $+1.5 d_{EM}$. Add a nice color bar, which will again probably need to be logarithmic. Mark the location of the earth with a black circle, and the moon with a black square. Add a legend.
In [9]:
ME = 5.9E24
M_Apollo = 5500
xxx = np.linspace(-1.5*dEM,1.5*dEM,501)
yyy = np.linspace(-1.5*dEM,1.5*dEM,501)
x_grid3, y_grid3 = np.meshgrid(xxx,yyy)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
FxE, FyE = Grav_Force(ME,M_Apollo,0,0,x_grid3,y_grid3)
FxM, FyM = Grav_Force(MM,M_Apollo,dEM/np.sqrt(2),dEM/np.sqrt(2),x_grid3,y_grid3)
Fx=FxE+FxM
Fy=FyE+FyM
colors = np.log(np.hypot(Fx,Fy))

stream = ax.streamplot(x_grid3,y_grid3,Fx,Fy,color=colors,cmap="viridis")
cbar = fig.colorbar(stream.lines)
p1 = ax.plot(0,0,"o",color="black", label="Earth")
p2 = ax.plot(dEM/np.sqrt(2), dEM/np.sqrt(2),"s", color="black", label="Moon")
cbar.set_label("ln(Gravitational Force) [N]")
ax.set_xlabel("x position [m]")
ax.set_ylabel("y position [m]")
ax.set_title("Gravitational Force Vectors in the Earth Moon System")
ax.legend(loc='lower left')

plt.tight_layout()

C:\Users\danya\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_73012\4255258327.py:32: RuntimeWarning: divide by zero encountered in divide
  mag_f = G*M1*M2/r**2
  1. You will note there is a point between the Earth and the Moon where the field lines seem to vanish. This is the point where the Earth's and Moon's gravitational forces cancel eachother. By setting $|\vec{F}_{moon}| = |\vec{F}_{earth}|$, calculate the radial distance of the "balance point." From there, identify the $x$ and $y$ coordinates of the balance point. Typeset your derivation (show multiple steps!) in latex in a markdown cell below. Then, calculate it numerically, and draw the answer onto a new version of your force field plot. Again mark the location of the earth with a black circle, and the moon with a black square; mark the location of the balance point with a red x. Add a legend.

Your Derivation Goes in the markdown cell below:

We begin with: $$|\vec{F}_{moon}| = |\vec{F}_{earth}|$$ Substituting in, we obtain: $$-G\frac{M_{moon}\cdot M_{apollo}}{(x-x_m)^2+(y-y_m)^2} = -G\frac{M_{earth}\cdot M_{apollo}}{(x^2+y^2)}$$ Canceling out terms on both sides, we get: $$\frac{M_{moon}}{(x-x_m)^2+(y-y_m)^2} = \frac{M_{earth}}{x^2+y^2}$$ To solve, note that $x_m=y_m$ so for $\hat{r}_{moon}=(x_m-x,y_m-y)$ and $\hat{r}_{earth}=(x,y)$ to cancel, our point $(x,y)$ must lie on the line $x=y$. Substituting in, we obtain: $$\frac{M_{moon}}{(x-x_m)^2} = \frac{M_{earth}}{x^2}$$ Solving, we get: $$M_{moon}x^2 = M_{earth}(x-x_m)^2$$ $$x^2(-M_{moon}+M_{earth})-x(2x_mM_{earth})+x_m^2M_{earth} = 0$$ Finally, applying the quadratic formula, we obtain: $$x = \frac{2x_mM_{earth}\pm \sqrt{4x_m^2M_{earth}^2-4x_m^2M_{earth}(M_{earth}-M_{moon})}}{2(M_{earth}-M_{moon})}=\frac{x_mM_{earth}\pm x_m\sqrt{M_{earth}M_{moon}}}{M_{earth}-M_{moon}}$$ Note that the $+$ case is not what we want, as it represents a point where the force magnitudes are equal but pointing in the same direction. We therefore want the solution: $$x=\frac{x_mM_{earth}-x_m\sqrt{M_{earth}M_{moon}}}{M_{earth}-M_{moon}}$$

And your updated plot, including the balance point, goes in the cell below.

In [10]:
x_balance = (dEM/np.sqrt(2)*(ME-np.sqrt(ME*MM)))/(ME-MM)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
FxE, FyE = Grav_Force(ME,M_Apollo,0,0,x_grid3,y_grid3)
FxM, FyM = Grav_Force(MM,M_Apollo,dEM/np.sqrt(2),dEM/np.sqrt(2),x_grid3,y_grid3)
Fx=FxE+FxM
Fy=FyE+FyM
colors = np.log(np.hypot(Fx,Fy))

stream = ax.streamplot(x_grid3,y_grid3,Fx,Fy,color=colors,cmap="viridis")
cbar = fig.colorbar(stream.lines)
p1 = ax.plot(0,0,"o",color="black", label='Earth')
p2 = ax.plot(dEM/np.sqrt(2), dEM/np.sqrt(2),"s", color="black", label='Moon')
p3 = ax.plot(x_balance,x_balance,"rx", label='balance point')
cbar.set_label("ln(Gravitational Force) [N]")
ax.set_xlabel("x position [m]")
ax.set_ylabel("y position [m]")
ax.set_title("Gravitational Force Vectors in the Earth Moon System -- Balance Point")
ax.legend(loc='lower left')

plt.tight_layout()

C:\Users\danya\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_73012\4255258327.py:32: RuntimeWarning: divide by zero encountered in divide
  mag_f = G*M1*M2/r**2
  1. Quantitatively discuss your conclusions. Is the balance point close to the Moon or Earth? How close? Does this make sense?

Your Answer Goes in the markdown cell below:

The balance point is very close to the moon. This makes a lot of sense, given that the moon is far less massive than the earth and therefore exerts far less gravitational force on a given object than the Earth does. In order to balance out the strength of the Earth's gravitational pull, the object, in this case the Apollo spacecraft, must be very close to the moon.

Part 4: Altitude of the Saturn V Rocket¶

Rockets function by conservation of momentum. The ejection of fuel backwards propels the rocket forwards. The change in a rocket's velocity $\Delta v$ as a function of time $t$ (under a set of simplifying assumptions) is given by the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation:

$$\Delta v(t) = v_e \ln \left(\frac{m_0}{m(t)}\right) - gt$$

where $m_0$ is the initial "wet" mass (fuel + rocket parts + payload), $m(t) = m_0 - \dot{m}t$ is the mass at time $t$, $\dot{m}$ is the fuel burn rate (which we will assume to be constant), $v_e$ is the fuel exhaust velocity, and $g$ is the gravitational acceleration.

You can find the altitude $h$ of the rocket at "burnout" (when all the fuel is used up) by integrating the velocity over time:

$$h = \int_{0}^{T} \Delta v(t) \, dt$$

where $T$ is the total burn time of the rocket, which by conservation of momentum arguments is:

$$T = \frac{m_0 - m_f}{\dot{m}}$$

where $m_f$ is the final "dry mass" of the rocket, once all the fuel is burned.

  1. Calculate the burn time $T$ for the first stage of the new Saturn V rocket that NASA is planning to use to carry the Apollo capsule.
In [11]:
m0 = 2.8E6
mf = 7.5E5
mdot = 1.3E4
T = (m0-mf)/mdot
print(f"The burn time of the first stage is {T:.6} seconds")

The burn time of the first stage is 157.692 seconds
  1. Define a function that returns the change in velocity $\Delta v(t)$. It should take all necessary arguments, meaning: the time, wet mass, dry mass, burn rate, exhaust speed, and gravitational constant $g$. Have it return the change in velocity. Note that the rocket eventually runs out of fuel, so you should design your function carefully so that $\Delta v$ eventually becomes zero after all fuel is spent. Again, document and guard your function according to best practices.
In [12]:
def velocity_change(t : (float,int,np.ndarray),v_e : (float,int),m_0 : (float,int),m_f : (float,int),m_dot : (float,int) ,g: (float,int)) -> float:
    """A function which calculates the change in velocity due to the ejection of fuel backwards from a rocket, based on the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation

    Args:
        t (float, int, or np.ndarray): the time
        v_e (float or int): the fuel exhaust velocity
        m_0 (float or int): wet mass of the rocket
        m_f (float or int): dry mass of the rocket
        m_dot (float or int): the burn rate of the rocket
        g (float, int): the acceleration due to gravity

    Returns:
        float: the x-component of the gravitational force between the two objects
    """

    if not isinstance(t, (float, int, np.ndarray)):
        raise TypeError(f"First mass input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(v_e, (float, int)):
        raise TypeError(f"Second mass input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(m_0, (float, int)):
        raise TypeError(f"First x-coordinate input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(m_f, (float, int)):
        raise TypeError(f"First y-coordinate input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(m_dot, (float,int)):
        raise TypeError(f"Second x-coordinate input is of the wrong type!")
    elif not isinstance(g, (float,int)):
        raise TypeError(f"Second y-coordinate input is of the wrong type!")
    else:
        m_t = m_0-m_dot*t
        delt_v = ve*np.log(m_0/m_t)-g*t
        d_v = np.where(np.isclose(m_t,m_f),0,delt_v)
        return d_v
  1. Compute the altitude of the rocket at the end of the burn. Do this by numerically integrating from $t=0$ (launch) to $t=T$ (burnout). Use the scipy.integrate.quad() function.
In [13]:
from scipy.integrate import quad
ve = 2.4E3
a_g = 9.81
integral, err = quad(velocity_change,0,T,args=(ve,m0,mf,mdot,a_g))
print(f"The altitude of the rocket will be {integral/1000:.4} kilometers above sea level.")

The altitude of the rocket will be 74.09 kilometers above sea level.
  1. Just last week, NASA got the test results back from the first prototype of Saturn V. They found that it burned for about 160 seconds and lifted the system to an altitude of about 70km. Director Kranz has asked you to compare your calculations for $T$ and $h$ to the testing data. You should offer some explanations about why your calculation is an over or under estimate, e.g. neglecting drag.

Your Answer Goes in the markdown cell below:

The result for t is a slight underestimate, off by under 2.5 seconds or 1.4%. This is probably due to somewhat uneven burn rates as the rocket moves through the atmosphere. For example, the burn rate efficiency may decrease as the rocket runs due to vibrational damage to components and other problems, leading to an increased burn time. The result for the height on the other hand, is a substantial overestimate (by about 4km or 5%). This is likely due to our faliure to account for drag (a major issue below about 10km, or roughly the first 60 seconds of the flight), and perhaps a lower acceleration from the rocket fuel burning itself given the reduced burn rate described above.

In [ ]:
